skill.name: ir-velociraptor
skill.description: Guides authorized Velociraptor incident response, endpoint forensics, evidence collection, and VQL-based threat hunting.
skill.summary: Create Velociraptor hunts, collectors, and forensic queries for authorized endpoint investigations.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly explained by the skill being a Velociraptor DFIR guide, not by hidden malicious code. However, the content includes templates for broad endpoint collection, credential-adjacent artifact discovery, privileged service deployment, webhook notification, and shell-based installation patterns, so publication should require human review and strong warnings.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Endpoint and Credential-Adjacent Collection
high_findings.description: The skill provides offline collector and MITRE mapping examples that can gather endpoint process, network, persistence, event log, file timeline, SAM hive, and browser credential-store evidence. This is expected for authorized DFIR, but it is high risk because misuse can expose sensitive host and user data.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files explicitly describe sensitive evidence collection and warn that VQL can collect credentials, PII, and sensitive files. Legitimate incident response use is clear, but the data exposure risk is real.

high_findings.title: Privileged Deployment and Persistence-Like Service Instructions
high_findings.description: Deployment guidance includes sudo commands, systemd service creation and enablement, Windows service installation, firewall changes, and NFS storage configuration. These are normal for Velociraptor administration but can create persistent privileged endpoint or server components if used outside approved scope.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are administrative deployment steps rather than covert persistence. The risk remains high because they modify privileged services and host network controls.

high_findings.title: Pipe-to-Shell Install Pattern in CI Template
high_findings.description: The CI template installs tfsec with curl piped directly to bash. This pattern executes remote content without pinning or verification and was correctly flagged as dangerous, even though it appears in a template rather than active runtime code.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line directly pipes a downloaded installer to bash. The context is a GitHub Actions template, but the supply-chain risk is concrete if copied into production CI.

medium_findings.title: Broad Hunt and Command Execution Templates
medium_findings.description: The hunt template and quick-start examples include Velociraptor commands that create hunts, monitor results, and export endpoint data. These are legitimate DFIR workflows, but mistakes in target labels, parameters, or authorization can collect data at enterprise scale.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and operational. The examples do not show malicious intent, so medium severity is appropriate when paired with the high-risk data collection findings.

medium_findings.title: Webhook, URL, and Example Network Endpoints
medium_findings.description: Several network findings are placeholders or documentation links, including a Slack webhook placeholder and example URLs. These are mostly safe as examples, but users could accidentally commit real webhook URLs or route investigation data to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are placeholders or public documentation in the reviewed snippets. The remaining risk is operational leakage if users replace placeholders with real secrets in shared templates.

medium_findings.title: CI Token and Filesystem Access in Workflow Template
medium_findings.description: The CI workflow uses the GitHub token for security scanning and reads a generated report before posting a pull request comment. This is a common workflow pattern, but it requires least-privilege permissions and careful handling of report content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token and filesystem access are visible and semantically legitimate for GitHub Actions. Risk depends on repository permissions and whether report content can contain untrusted text.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Many weak cryptography alerts are caused by security-rule examples, artifact metadata, or text about hashing. The reviewed artifact template uses SHA256, and the security rule template explains weak crypto detection rather than using weak algorithms in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context shows documentation and detection rules rather than executable weak cryptographic operations. Other static weak-crypto hits should still be treated as review signals in this security domain.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Keywords Are Expected For Forensic Queries
low_findings.description: System, network, registry, and USB enumeration queries are core Velociraptor forensic techniques. They are not hidden reconnaissance behavior in the skill itself, but they should run only within an authorized investigation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The query intent is clearly defensive evidence collection. The risk is contextual misuse, not a false claim about the files containing enumeration logic.

low_findings.title: Educational Vulnerability Examples Trigger Script and Secret Rules
low_findings.description: The example reference includes intentionally vulnerable XSS snippets and secure API-key handling examples. These are educational examples, not active application code executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside markdown examples that explain vulnerable and safer patterns. They do not create a runtime XSS or secret-exfiltration path in this skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The CI template uses a curl-to-bash installer pattern that can execute unverified remote code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct curl-to-bash evidence exists at the cited line. This is a known supply-chain risk if copied unchanged.

dangerous_patterns.title: Offline Collector for Broad Endpoint Evidence
dangerous_patterns.description: The offline collector configuration collects many host artifacts, including processes, network connections, persistence data, event logs, timelines, prefetch data, and USB device history.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The collector artifact list is explicit. This is legitimate for DFIR but high impact if run without approval.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Store and SAM Hive Discovery Patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: MITRE mapping examples include SAM hive paths and browser login database paths for credential-access investigations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples reference sensitive credential-adjacent files. They are detection and access-review queries, not credential theft code, but data sensitivity is high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Service Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: Deployment instructions create and enable Linux systemd services and install a Windows service for Velociraptor clients.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete service installation steps. They are expected for Velociraptor operations but require administrator control.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Notification Webhook Placeholder
dangerous_patterns.description: The hunt template includes a Slack webhook placeholder for match notifications.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The value is a placeholder, so there is no embedded secret. The pattern is still relevant because real webhook use can leak investigation metadata.

content.value_statement: Incident responders need fast endpoint visibility during active investigations. This skill provides Velociraptor VQL patterns, collector guidance, and hunt templates for authorized forensic work.
actual_capabilities: Explains Velociraptor collect, monitor, and hunt workflows for incident response.
Provides VQL examples for process, network, file system, registry, and timeline investigations.
Includes templates for hunts, custom artifacts, offline collectors, and security scan CI workflows.
Maps investigation queries to MITRE ATT&CK techniques and common response scenarios.
Documents deployment, access control, evidence handling, and operational safety considerations.
limitations: Requires an existing Velociraptor environment or local binary to run queries.
Does not verify user authorization or legal approval before endpoint collection.
Examples may need tuning for each operating system, artifact version, and network design.
Some guidance can collect sensitive data and must be restricted to approved investigations.

use_cases.title: Triage Active Incidents
use_cases.description: Build a scoped Velociraptor collection plan for affected endpoints, then review process, network, event log, and file timeline evidence.

use_cases.title: Develop Threat Hunts
use_cases.description: Create VQL hunts for suspicious PowerShell, persistence, lateral movement, credential access indicators, and unusual network activity.

use_cases.title: Prepare Forensic Collection
use_cases.description: Design an offline collector with resource limits, chain-of-custody notes, and evidence protection controls for approved investigations.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Collection
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Velociraptor collection plan for one Windows endpoint after suspicious login activity. Include scope, artifacts, safety limits, and expected outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Focused VQL Hunt
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a VQL hunt for suspicious PowerShell execution across production Windows clients. Include parameters, triage fields, and false positive review steps.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Offline Collector
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an offline Velociraptor collector for a ransomware investigation. Limit collection to approved artifacts, set resource controls, and include chain-of-custody handling.

prompt_templates.title: Map Findings to ATT&CK
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Velociraptor findings and map them to MITRE ATT&CK tactics. Separate confirmed evidence, assumptions, and recommended follow-up collections.

output_examples.input: Request: plan initial collection for three suspected compromised laptops.
output_examples.output: A scoped collection checklist covering authentication logs, process history, network connections, persistence points, timeline data, evidence storage, and approval notes.

output_examples.input: Request: hunt for suspicious encoded PowerShell activity.
output_examples.output: A VQL hunt outline with target labels, command-line indicators, resource limits, triage priority, expected false positives, and review workflow.

output_examples.input: Request: summarize Velociraptor evidence for an executive incident update.
output_examples.output: A plain-language report describing affected hosts, observed behaviors, confidence level, open questions, and recommended containment actions.

best_practices: Run hunts only on approved client labels and document the investigation scope before collection.
Minimize sensitive data collection and encrypt evidence archives during transfer and storage.
Test VQL in a lab or notebook before running it across production endpoints.
anti_patterns: Do not run broad enterprise hunts without resource limits and change approval.
Do not collect credential stores, browser data, or registry hives unless the case requires them.
Do not paste webhook URLs, API keys, or private keys into shared artifacts or reports.

faq.question: Does this skill run Velociraptor by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and templates. Users still need their own Velociraptor deployment, permissions, and review process.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production investigations?
faq.answer: Yes, for authorized teams with Velociraptor expertise. Production use needs scoping, access control, logging, and resource limits.

faq.question: Can it help create custom VQL artifacts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes artifact structure guidance and examples for common forensic collection patterns.

faq.question: Does it include MITRE ATT&CK mapping?
faq.answer: Yes. Reference material maps common Velociraptor hunts to ATT&CK tactics and techniques.

faq.question: What data can the examples collect?
faq.answer: Examples cover processes, network connections, event logs, registry keys, file timelines, services, browser artifacts, and other endpoint evidence.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The templates can support sensitive endpoint collection. They should be used only under approved incident response procedures.
